capabilities: Balance reliability and innovation using explicit error budgets.

procedure.action: Specify service level objectives (SLOs) by identifying key performance indicators such as availability and latency; set thresholds using historical data and business requirements by specifying schema, constraints, and invariants in machine-readable form; include at least one falsifiable acceptance criterion where error_rate < 0.001.
Calculate the error budget by subtracting the SLO target from 100%; express this as a percentage of allowable downtime or errors over a defined period.
Establish an error budget policy by setting rules for development activities; restrict feature releases when error budget consumption exceeds 80% within the measurement period.

Enforce the error budget policy by integrating automated alerts in the CI/CD pipeline; trigger a release freeze when error budget usage surpasses the defined threshold using domain-appropriate tooling; assert error_count < 1 and result_valid = true; log operation_duration_ms and result_code to audit trail.

Observe error budget consumption using a telemetry pipeline; visualize real-time error budget usage with dashboards and alert on breaches by polling the target metric at interval_ms < 60000; emit METRIC_ALERT if value exceeds threshold for 3 consecutive samples; assert alert_latency < 5000ms.

summary: Define and enforce error budgets to balance system reliability with innovation, ensuring service level objectives are met without stifling development.
title: Error Budget Enforcement for Reliability

failureConditions: Service reliability degrades when error budgets are not enforced, causing excessive downtime and customer dissatisfaction.
Innovation is stifled when error budgets are too conservative, leading to missed opportunities and competitive disadvantage.
Operational chaos ensues when error budget policies are unclear, causing inconsistent enforcement and team confusion.
